headline: The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft: Where Champions Were Built and Dreams Went to Die
subheadline: Fourteen teams, 210 picks, one snake draft that felt like watching the Red Wedding unfold in slow motion—except with more spreadsheets and fewer Starks.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a moment in every fantasy draft—usually around pick 47, when someone takes a closer way too early and the chat explodes—where you realize this isn't just a hobby anymore. It's a lifestyle. It's a sickness. It's the reason your spouse knows who Cristopher Sánchez is even though they couldn't name three Supreme Court justices. Welcome to MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball, where we've been ruining friendships and marriages since before your favorite podcast existed.

The 2026 draft had everything: a defending champion (**Sugar Land Skeeters**) trying to run it back, a perennial basement dweller (**North Correa**) armed with the first overall pick and the desperation of a team that's finished 14th and 12th the past two years, and enough questionable mid-round decisions to make you wonder if some managers were drafting from a Cracker Barrel parking lot with one bar of service. The biggest steal? **Gunnar Henderson** falling to pick 17 like he was radioactive. The worst reach? We'll get there, but let's just say someone took **Corbin Carroll** in the first round despite him posting a .657 OPS in 2025. (This is the fantasy equivalent of ordering the fish at a steakhouse—technically you can do it, but why would you?)

Think of this draft as the fantasy baseball version of *Succession*—everyone thinks they're the smartest person in the room, alliances are meaningless, and in the end, someone's getting absolutely destroyed while convincing themselves they actually won. Let's break down who played Kendall (thought they were winning but absolutely weren't), who played Shiv (solid moves with fatal blind spots), and who's just straight-up Greg (bless their heart, they're trying).

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The draft order gods smiled upon **North Correa**, handing them the first overall pick after consecutive finishes of 14th and 12th. (That's called "earning it the hard way," folks.) They did exactly what you'd expect and took **Bobby Witt Jr.**, who slashed .332/.389/.592 with 32 homers and 31 steals in 2025—the kind of five-category monster that makes the first pick a no-brainer for once. The **Athletic Park Dragons** followed with **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)**, which is still the most video-game-cheat-code pick in fantasy baseball. The man hit .303 with 54 homers and a .975 OPS last year. FIFTY-FOUR HOMERS. As a BATTER ONLY. We're basically drafting a created player.

The first-round drama really heated up in the back half. **Sugar Land Skeeters**, fresh off their championship, snagged **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 11—a bet on upside that could look genius or disastrous by July. **Riverside Roughnecks** took **Jackson Chourio** at 12, banking on the 21-year-old's 25-homer, 22-steal season to be just the appetizer. And then **Canberra Wombats** rolled the dice on **Corbin Carroll** at 13, which... we need to talk about. The man hit .219/.308/.349 in 2025. That's a .657 OPS. That's what we in the business call "a problem." Bold swing, Wombats. Bold swing.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa (Pick #1)**
After back-to-back cellar finishes (14th in 2024, 12th in 2025), **North Correa** came into this draft like a team that had nothing to lose and everything to prove. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1.01 is the safest pick in fantasy—the guy is basically a cheat code with his .332 average, 32 homers, and 31 steals. The **Pete Alonso** grab at 28 adds 40-homer pop (he hit 34 in 2025 with a 119 wRC+), and **Logan Gilbert** at 29 gives them a true SP2 (3.09 ERA, 189 Ks last year). But here's where it gets spicy: **Spencer Strider** at 113 and **Sandy Alcantara** at 112 are both coming off major injuries. That's either brilliant late-round upside or a field hospital. **Luis Robert Jr.** at 141 is another injury lottery ticket. This team is basically screaming "we'll either finish first or we'll crash and burn spectacularly." I respect the chaos.
**Grade: B+** (Witt carries, but the injury gambles better pay off)

**Athletic Park Dragons (Pick #2)**
The Dragons plummeted from 6th to 14th last year, which is the fantasy equivalent of the 2023 Padres—everyone expected greatness and got heartbreak instead. **Shohei Ohtani** at 2 is obviously correct (54 HR, .975 OPS, 9.2 WAR as a BATTER), but the pitching strategy here is fascinating. They loaded up: **Michael King** (2.95 ERA, 1.12 WHIP), **Shota Imanaga** (2.91 ERA in his rookie year), **Aaron Nola**, **Luis Castillo**—that's four legitimate starters by pick 87. The problem? **Matt Olson** at 27 had a down year (.248, 104 wRC+), and **Kyle Schwarber** at 30 is the definition of "you're gonna hate his batting average." The **Kenley Jansen** pick at 198 feels like drafting with nostalgia goggles—he's 38 and his fastball isn't what it used to be.
**Grade: B** (Pitching depth is real, but the lineup has holes)

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers (Pick #3)**
A first-year team (only 2025 on record, finished 10th) that drafted like they'd been here before. **Mookie Betts** at 3 is elite multi-position eligibility plus a .307/.408/.535 slash—the man is a walking cheat code for categories leagues. **Ketel Marte** at 26 gives them another multi-eligible stud (120 wRC+ in 2025). Here's the thing: this team went HEAVY on relievers late. **Justin Martinez**, **Jeff Hoffman**, **Jason Foley**, **Jason Adam**—that's four closers/high-leverage arms in the final six rounds. It's either a brilliant NSVH/ratio strategy or they completely whiffed on starting pitching. **Christian Yelich** at 82 is a "remember when he won MVP?" pick that could look smart or sad by June. The **William Contreras** selection at 31 is sneaky good—best hitting catcher in baseball (133 wRC+ in 2025).
**Grade: B** (Bold strategy, Cotton. Let's see if it pays off.)

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Pick #4)**
The ONLY team in this league with a championship (2024) and they've been remarkably consistent: 8th, 1st, 6th. They know what they're doing. **Aaron Judge** at 4 is automatic—58 homers and a .322/.458/.689 slash in 2025. (That's a 1.147 OPS. That's not real. That's a video game.) **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** at 25 adds speed and chaos, **Austin Riley** at 32 provides the 30-homer floor. But the late rounds are where dynasties are built, and **Xavier Edwards** at 116 (35 steals in 2025!) and **Kevin Gausman** at 137 are the kind of value picks that win weeks. The only concern: **Spencer Schwellenbach** at 81 had a solid rookie year (3.22 ERA) but is he a true SP2 or a mirage?
**Grade: A-** (Championship DNA showing)

**BaseballPro77 (Pick #5)**
Back-to-back top-2 finishes in 2023-24 (including a title), then a slide to 7th last year. This is the team version of the Lakers after LeBron's first championship—still good, but something feels... off. **Elly De La Cruz** at 5 is the ultimate boom-or-bust pick: 31 homers, 67 steals, but also a .235 average and 155 strikeouts. You're betting on the tools. **Jackson Merrill** at 24 is a steal—.292 with 24 HR and a 3.5 WAR at age 21. But **Emmanuel Clase** at 52 in a roto league? That's early for a closer, even one with a 0.61 ERA. **Bo Bichette** at 145 is either a league-winning value or a guy who hit .225 with an 85 wRC+ last year. (It's the latter. It's definitely the latter.)
**Grade: B** (Former champ coasting on reputation)

**Albuquerque Bombers (Pick #6)**
The Bombers have been sneaky consistent: 5th, 10th, 3rd. They're like the perpetual 4-seed in the NCAA Tournament—always there, never quite breaking through. **José Ramírez** at 6 is rock-solid (116 wRC+, 28 HR, 20 SB), and **Freddie Freeman** at 23 just refuses to age (.302/.391/.476 at age 36). **Garrett Crochet** at 34 is the upside play—230 innings of potential if the Red Sox don't implode. But here's my favorite pick: **Willy Adames** at 79. The man hit 32 homers with a 123 wRC+ last year. That's a third-rounder falling to the sixth. The **Xander Bogaerts** pick at 90 feels like they're chasing 2018, but the late-round **Jasson Domínguez** (146) is a lottery ticket worth holding.
**Grade: B+** (Consistent, solid, unsexy—like a Toyota Camry)

**Hiroshima Radiation (Pick #7)**
The most volatile team in the league: 2nd, 11th, 5th over three years. This is the team that's either brilliant or incompetent and there's no in-between. **Juan Soto** at 7 is elite (.288/.419/.569, 41 HR, 7.6 WAR), and pairing him with **Trea Turner** at 22 (.295, 21 HR, 23 SB) gives them a legit 1-2 punch. But then they took **Tyler Glasnow** at 63 and **Robbie Ray** at 134—two arms with injury histories longer than most CVS receipts. **Jose Altuve** at 50 is a "trust the track record" play that could backfire (he hit .234 with a 94 wRC+ in 2025). The **Blake Snell** pick at 35 is high-risk/high-reward: when he's on, he's a Cy Young winner; when he's off, he's walking the bases loaded in the second inning.
**Grade: B-** (Soto can't carry this alone)

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Pick #8)**
Three straight top-5 finishes (4th, 5th, 2nd). This is the "always a bridesmaid" team, and this draft screams "we're tired of losing in the finals." **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at 8 had a monster 2025 (.323/.396/.544, 30 HR), and **Bryce Harper** at 21 is still Bryce freaking Harper (.309/.406/.572). **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at 64 is the steal of the draft if healthy—we're talking about a guy who hit .337 with 41 steals in 2023 falling to the fifth round because of injury concerns. **Devin Williams** at 92 gives them an elite closer. The **Corbin Burnes** pick at 36 anchors the rotation (3.39 ERA, 194 Ks). This team is built to win NOW.
**Grade: A** (This is their year or bust)

**Buttville Skidmarks (Pick #9)**
I'm just going to acknowledge the team name here. (Incredible. No notes.) The Skidmarks have been stuck in neutral: 11th, 8th, 8th. **Kyle Tucker** at 9 is the foundation—.289/.395/.585 with 112 RBI in his first Dodgers season. **Zack Wheeler** at 20 is an ace (2.57 ERA, 0.96 WHIP). **Rafael Devers** at 37 mashes (.296, 33 HR, 118 wRC+). But here's the gamble: **Jacob deGrom** at 65. The man hasn't thrown a healthy full season since Biden's first year. They're betting he's finally back—at age 38. Also, **Nolan Arenado** at 205? That's a former MVP candidate as a 15th-rounder. Either he's washed or this is grand larceny.
**Grade: B+** (deGrom is the swing factor—pun intended)

**General Hospital (Pick #10)**
Back-to-back 13th-place finishes. This team's name is now ironic. **Francisco Lindor** at 10 is fine (.273, 33 HR, 29 SB, 7.6 WAR), but **Mike Trout** at 75 is... concerning. We're drafting hope at this point. (And yes, I just called Mike Trout a hope pick. This is where we are in 2026.) **Jarren Duran** at 19 is legit value—.285 with 21 HR and 34 SB. **Chris Sale** at 38 just won the Cy Young (2.38 ERA, 0.92 WHIP) at 37 years old, because baseball is absurd. The **Bryan Woo** pick at 103 is sneaky—3.14 ERA with elite control. But this roster feels like it's one injury away from disaster.
**Grade: C+** (Trout pick is a prayer, not a strategy)

**Sugar Land Skeeters (Pick #11)**
DEFENDING CHAMPIONS. Went 13th, 3rd, 1st—a legitimate dynasty in the making. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 11 is the upside bet (.285, 27 HR, 21 SB when healthy). **Tarik Skubal** at 18 is the best pitcher in baseball right now (2.39 ERA, 2.54 FIP, 228 Ks). **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** at 46 anchors the rotation. But here's my favorite move: **Brenton Doyle** at 67. The guy hit .279 with 24 HR and 32 SB—in COORS. That's real. The **Cody Bellinger** pick at 39 is the bounce-back candidate (he hit .266 with 18 HR... fine, but not 2019 MVP Belli). This team drafted like champions.
**Grade: A-** (Knows how to win)

**Riverside Roughnecks (Pick #12)**
Three straight years of 9th, 9th, 11th. That's impressively mediocre. But this draft could change everything. **Jackson Chourio** at 12 is the right bet—25 HR, 22 SB, 2.8 WAR at age 21. And then **Gunnar Henderson** FELL TO THEM AT 17. GUNNAR HENDERSON. The guy hit .282 with 37 homers and 5.9 WAR. That's a top-5 pick sliding to 17. I don't know what the other teams were doing, but the Roughnecks just robbed a bank in broad daylight. **Roki Sasaki** at 96 is the Japanese phenom finally in MLB—if he's healthy, that's an SP1 in round 7. **Manny Machado** at 45 adds veteran stability.
**Grade: A** (Henderson fall is inexcusable for everyone else)

**Canberra Wombats (Pick #13)**
The Wombats have been inconsistent: 3rd, 12th, 4th. And this draft is... a choice. **Corbin Carroll** at 13 is the pick that will define their season. The guy hit .219/.308/.349 in 2025. A .657 OPS. They're betting on the 2023 version (.285/.368/.514, 25 HR, 54 SB) coming back. It's a MASSIVE gamble. **Yordan Alvarez** at 16 is the safety net—.297/.392/.560, 35 HR. **Josh Hader** at 69 gives them elite saves. But **CJ Abrams** at 44 had a 97 wRC+ last year, and **Wyatt Langford** at 41 disappointed in his rookie season (.250, 13 HR). Too many "they'll figure it out" picks.
**Grade: C+** (Carroll pick could look historically bad)

**Benchwarmers (Pick #14)**
Finished 14th in 2023, then climbed to 7th and 9th. Progress! **Julio Rodríguez** at 14 had a down year (.275, 20 HR, 25 SB) but he's still only 25. **Paul Skenes** at 15 is the real prize—the rookie phenom (1.96 ERA, 11.0 K/9 in his debut) falling to the second round because of innings concerns. **Oneil Cruz** at 42 is the ultimate high-variance play (.271, 20 HR, 26 SB, but also 177 strikeouts). **Dylan Cease** at 43 adds 200+ innings of strikeouts. The **Jake Burger** pick at 98 is sneaky—30 HR potential at a thin position. This team swung for the fences on upside.
**Grade: B+** (Skenes + Julio could be special)

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE HEROES (Best Value Picks):**

1. **Gunnar Henderson to Riverside Roughnecks (Round 2, Pick 17)** — This is the steal of the entire draft. Henderson hit .282/.364/.519 with 37 homers and 5.9 WAR last year. He's a consensus top-5 pick who fell to 17. Twelve teams passed on him. TWELVE. This is like the Patriots getting Tom Brady at 199, except everyone KNEW Henderson was good.

2. **Ronald Acuña Jr. to Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Round 5, Pick 64)** — Yes, the injuries are scary. Yes, he missed most of 2024 and struggled in 2025. But this is a former MVP with 40-40 potential falling to the FIFTH ROUND. If he's 80% of his peak, that's a league-winner at pick 64.

3. **Xavier Edwards to Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Round 9, Pick 116)** — 35 steals. THIRTY-FIVE STEALS. In a categories league, that's a cheat code in round 9. The Twins' championship experience is showing.

**THE VILLAINS (Worst Reaches):**

1. **Corbin Carroll to Canberra Wombats (Round 1, Pick 13)** — A .657 OPS in the first round. I don't care about the 2023 season—you draft based on what happened LAST YEAR. Yordan Alvarez was sitting right there at 16. Henderson went at 17. This is a catastrophic reach based on vibes and hope.

2. **Emmanuel Clase to BaseballPro77 (Round 4, Pick 52)** — A closer at pick 52 is early for ANY categories league. Yes, his 0.61 ERA was absurd. But closers are fungible and you passed on hitters who could've anchored multiple categories.

3. **Mike Trout to General Hospital (Round 6, Pick 75)** — I love Mike Trout. We all love Mike Trout. But the man has played more than 119 games ONCE since 2019. This isn't a draft pick; it's a wish on a shooting star. At pick 75, you can't afford wishes.

**V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **North Correa will finish top-3 after consecutive basement finishes.** Bobby Witt Jr. is THAT good, and if Spencer Strider comes back healthy (big if), this team has sneaky upside. The pendulum swings.

2. **Corbin Carroll will NOT finish as a top-100 fantasy player, and Canberra Wombats will miss the playoffs.** The swing-and-miss issues aren't fixed, and you can't overcome a first-round pick posting replacement-level numbers.

3. **Ronald Acuña Jr. will have a top-20 fantasy season, making Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs look like geniuses.** The knee is finally right. Book it.

4. **Bo Bichette will be dropped by July, and BaseballPro77 will regret the pick 145 selection.** His batted ball data is broken. The swing isn't working. This isn't a slump; it's a decline.

5. **Roki Sasaki will have the best ERA among all pitchers drafted after pick 80.** The stuff is electric, the Dodgers will manage his workload perfectly, and Riverside Roughnecks will look like time travelers.

**VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Give me the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**. 

Three straight top-5 finishes without a title means they're due, and this draft is their magnum opus. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** and **Bryce Harper** form the most potent 1-2 punch in the first two rounds. **Corbin Burnes** anchors a rotation that includes **Framber Valdez** and late-round gems like **George Kirby** and **Seth Lugo**. The **Ronald Acuña Jr.** gamble is exactly the kind of swing a contender SHOULD take—high risk, league-winning reward. **Devin Williams** locks down NSVH. **Marcus Semien** provides steals from the middle infield.

The Wonderdogs drafted like a team that's tired of being good and wants to be great. They have the lineup depth to survive injuries, the pitching to dominate ratios, and the late-round upside to steal categories. They're the 2016 Cubs—a team that kept knocking on the door until they finally kicked it down.

**Sugar Land Skeeters** will push them. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** have championship DNA. But when October rolls around and the beer's flowing, it'll be the Wonderdogs hoisting the trophy, finally completing their three-year journey from bridesmaid to bride.

(And **North Correa**? After years in the basement, they're about to find out that hope is a beautiful thing. Maybe the best of things. And no good thing ever dies. Unless Spencer Strider's arm falls off again. Then all bets are off.)

See you at the championship.

*— BS*
